requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'PAT: Accelerating LLM Decoding via Prefix-Aware Attention with Resource Efficient Multi-Tile Kernel' have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment and infrastructure for building and running PAT have been set up.
Code has been set up to build PAT on a system with CUDA >= 12.4, PyTorch 2.7.0, and NVIDIA driver >= 550 (matching the reference impl's setup.py).

verification.spec: Inspect setup.py / pyproject.toml / README to confirm pinned dependencies: CUDA >= 12.4, torch==2.7.0, and that build instructions reference NVIDIA driver >= 550. Run `nvcc --version`, `python -c 'import torch; print(torch.__version__, torch.version.cuda)'`, and `nvidia-smi` to confirm the environment meets these versions.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up so PAT can be compiled against the CUTLASS/CuTe library as a dependency (mirroring the CUTLASS_ROOT build flow in the reference impl).

verification.spec: Confirm that setup.py honors a CUTLASS_ROOT environment variable and includes CUTLASS/CuTe include paths during compilation. Run `CUTLASS_ROOT=/path/to/cutlass pip install . --no-build-isolation` against a checked-out NVIDIA CUTLASS repository and verify the build succeeds and links against cutlass headers.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to target NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB and NVIDIA H100-SXM-80GB GPU architectures.

verification.spec: Inspect setup.py / CMakeLists / build flags to confirm sm_80 (A100) and sm_90 (H100) are present in the NVCC `-gencode arch=compute_XX,code=sm_XX` flags. Build the extension and run `cuobjdump --list-elf <built .so>` to confirm SASS for sm_80 and sm_90.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up so PAT is installable as a vLLM (v0.9.0) attention backend plugin, activated by setting VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN (and VLLM_USE_V1=0) as in the reference impl plugin/ directory.

verification.spec: Install PAT into a vLLM v0.9.0 environment. Launch `VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN VLLM_USE_V1=0 vllm serve <small-model> --enable-prefix-caching --enforce-eager` and verify (via vLLM logs) that the attention backend resolves to the PAT/PREFIX_ATTN plugin rather than the default backend.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to expose the PAT kernel API to Python via pybind11 (as in the reference impl prefix_attn package).

verification.spec: Import the prefix_attn (PAT) Python package and verify that the kernel entry points (e.g., the multi-tile attention forward function) are exposed via pybind11 bindings. Check that the compiled extension module is importable and that the bound functions are callable from Python.

sub_tasks.requirements: Models and datasets used in evaluation are acquired.
Code has been set up to load the Meta-Llama-3-8B model for end-to-end serving experiments.

verification.spec: Load meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B via vLLM v0.9.0 with VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN and verify the server starts, performs a single decode step, and returns a valid generation.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to load the Qwen3-8B model for end-to-end serving experiments.

verification.spec: Load Qwen/Qwen3-8B via vLLM v0.9.0 with VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN and verify the server starts, performs a single decode step, and returns a valid generation.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to load the Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct model used in the distributed (TP=2, PP=2) experiment on four A100 GPUs.

verification.spec: Load Qwen/Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct via vLLM v0.9.0 with tensor_parallel=2 and pipeline_parallel=2 on 4xA100 80GB. Verify the model loads, generates output, and uses the PAT attention backend.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to load the Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE model for the MoE evaluation on one A100.

verification.spec: Load Qwen/Qwen3-30B-A3B (MoE) via vLLM v0.9.0 on a single A100 80GB with VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN. Verify the model loads and produces a valid generation.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to load the toolagent real-world trace (tool/agent workload with task-specific system prompts; overall cache hit rate 59%).

verification.spec: Load the toolagent trace and verify the resulting request stream contains tool/agent system prompts and that the empirical KV prefix cache hit rate computed over the first 30 minutes is approximately 59%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to load the conversation trace combining the Meta-AI system instruction with burstgpt prompts.

verification.spec: Load the conversation trace constructor that combines the Meta-AI system instruction with burstgpt prompts. Verify the trace produces requests whose prefixes include the Meta-AI system instruction text and whose body comes from burstgpt prompts.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to use only the first 30 minutes of each trace for end-to-end evaluation.

verification.spec: Inspect the trace loader / benchmark driver to confirm requests are filtered to only those arriving within the first 30 minutes of the trace timeline. Run the loader on a sample trace and verify max(timestamp) <= 1800s.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to construct the conversation trace's three-level shared prefix by randomizing language and country fields in the system instruction (lengths 46, 348, 2123 for Llama3 tokenizer and 45, 351, 2126 for Qwen3 tokenizer).

verification.spec: Run the conversation-trace construction script (combining Meta-AI system instruction with burstgpt prompts, randomizing language/country). Tokenize the three-level shared prefix with the Llama3 tokenizer and assert the three prefix-level lengths are exactly [46, 348, 2123] tokens; tokenize with Qwen3 tokenizer and assert [45, 351, 2126]. Report the level-3 length (Llama3) as the measured value.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to filter ultra-long requests beyond Llama-3-8B's 8K context length limit while keeping Qwen3-8B's 32K context length.

verification.spec: Run the trace loader for Llama-3-8B and verify all returned requests have total token length <= 8192. Run it for Qwen3-8B and verify all requests have total length <= 32768. Confirm that requests longer than 8K are removed for Llama-3-8B but retained for Qwen3-8B.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Pack stage of PAT (prefix-aware pack scheduler) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented to convert vLLM's two-dimensional paged KV block table for a decode batch into a tree-structured (forest) block table, where each internal node represents a shared KV prefix with attributes l (KV-cache length of the shared prefix) and s (number of queries sharing it), and each leaf corresponds to one query.

verification.spec: Unit test: construct a synthetic vLLM-style 2D block table for a batch of queries with known multi-level shared prefixes (e.g., 4 queries sharing block IDs [0,1,3,6], [0,1,3,7], [0,2,4], [0,2,5]). Invoke PAT's tree-building API and assert the returned forest has internal nodes whose (l, s) match the expected shared-prefix length (in tokens) and query count, and that each leaf's root-to-leaf path reconstructs the original query's block list.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the pack scheduler to operate only on logical block IDs produced by vLLM's paged KV cache (no copy of KV data into a new layout).

verification.spec: Inspect the PAT vLLM integration code to confirm the pack scheduler takes vLLM's paged-KV block_tables (logical block IDs) as input and does not allocate or copy KV-cache tensor data into a new layout. Verify no torch.copy_/cat over KV cache occurs in the scheduler path.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the intra-node profit model: packing s_u queries sharing a prefix of length l_u into a single CTA saves (s_u - 1) * l_u * d global memory accesses and incurs 8 * s_u * d intermediate read/write overhead, giving the profit ratio described in Equation (1) of the paper.

verification.spec: Locate the intra-node profit computation in the pack scheduler source (e.g., profit model in pack scheduler module). Unit-test the function with synthetic inputs: for s_u=4, l_u=128, d=128, assert profit = (s_u-1)*l_u*d = 49152 and overhead = 8*s_u*d = 4096. Vary s_u in {2,4,8} and l_u in {16,64,256} and verify the returned profit/overhead matches the closed-form (s_u-1)*l_u*d and 8*s_u*d exactly.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the inter-node profit model's Scheme 1 (split): each child v_i of node u is packed in its own CTA separate from u, and the profit follows the per-node intra-node formula.

verification.spec: Construct a synthetic prefix tree node u with children {v_i}. Invoke the inter-node profit function in Scheme 1 mode and verify it returns the value sum over nodes using the intra-node formula (s-1)*l*d - 8*s*d for each separately-packed node, matching Equation for Scheme 1 in Section 5.1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the inter-node profit model's Scheme 2 (merge): child v_i is packed together with parent u into a single CTA, eliminating their intermediate results, with profit (s_i - 1)(l_u + l_i) d for the merged part.

verification.spec: Construct a synthetic prefix tree node u (with l_u, s_u) and child v_i (with l_i, s_i). Invoke Scheme 2 of the inter-node profit function and verify the returned profit for the merged part equals (s_i - 1) * (l_u + l_i) * d, with parent's residual computed using s_u' = s_u - s_i.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to select between Scheme 1 and Scheme 2 using the rule 'merge when 4 * s_i > l_u, otherwise split' as derived from the incremental profit 4*s_i*d - l_u*d.

verification.spec: Identify the scheme-selection branch in TreeHeuristic (Algorithm 1 line 7). Construct synthetic parent/child node pairs and verify: (a) when 4*s_i > l_u (e.g., s_i=64, l_u=16), the scheduler selects Scheme 2 (merge: child blocks concatenated with parent blocks passed to recursion, parent's queries reduced); (b) when 4*s_i < l_u (e.g., s_i=2, l_u=256), it selects Scheme 1 (split: child recursed with only child blocks, parent retains queries). Check both branches across at least 5 parameter combinations.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the TreeHeuristic algorithm (Algorithm 1): recursively scan children of each internal node, choose Scheme 1 or Scheme 2 according to the inter-node profit rule, recurse on children, and pack the node's remaining queries together with its blocks into a CTA.

verification.spec: Build the example tree from Figure 7 (4 queries Q1..Q4 with hierarchical KV-block prefixes). Call the implemented TreeHeuristic and verify: (1) the returned partition P covers every query exactly once across CTAs; (2) each CTA's KV blocks correspond to a contiguous root-to-leaf path segment in the tree; (3) the produced packing matches Scheme 1 vs Scheme 2 choices dictated by the 4*s_i vs l_u rule for each internal node. Also assert recursive traversal touches each node once (count visits).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the overall pack scheduler iterates each root of the tree-structured block-table forest, invokes TreeHeuristic, and unions the per-tree CTA partitions into the final partition P.

verification.spec: Build a forest with multiple roots from a synthetic block table. Run the overall pack scheduler and verify it calls TreeHeuristic per root and unions resulting CTA partitions into final partition P, with no missing or duplicate queries across CTAs.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the pack scheduler runs with linear complexity O(|V| + |E|) in the tree-structured block table (each node and edge visited once).

verification.spec: Instrument the pack scheduler with a node-visit counter. Run it on synthetic trees of total size N in {100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000} (balanced trees), and measure both runtime and visit count. Fit log(runtime) vs log(N): slope should be ~1.0 (linear). Assert each node and edge is visited exactly once (counter equals |V|+|E|). Report the fitted slope as the measured value.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the lazy-update mechanism that reuses a packing scheduling result across continuous-batching iterations until the block table changes (request arrivals/departures or new KV block assignments).

verification.spec: Run vLLM+PAT against a fixed decode batch for several iterations without changing the block table; instrument the scheduler to count invocations and verify the packing result is reused (only one packing computed across iterations). Then trigger a block-table change (simulated request arrival or new block allocation) and verify the scheduler re-runs and produces an updated packing.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so the pack scheduler is moved into the serving system and runs asynchronously on a CPU thread, overlapping with pre-attention tasks (metadata preparation, QKV projection), and is triggered once per several continuous-batching iterations rather than once per transformer layer.

verification.spec: Profile a vLLM decode iteration with PAT enabled using torch profiler or nsys; verify (a) the pack scheduler executes on a CPU thread distinct from the model-forward thread, (b) its execution overlaps with QKV projection / metadata preparation, and (c) it is invoked at most once across multiple transformer layers within the same batching iteration window.

sub_tasks.requirements: The multi-tile attention kernel (with tile-size selector) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented for a CUTLASS/CuTe-based tiled decode-attention kernel that processes packed CTAs with configurable Q tile size m and KV tile size n, producing per-query partial outputs (max score, log-sum-exp accumulator, partial value-weighted sum) to global memory for later merging.

verification.spec: Call PAT's tiled decode-attention kernel directly on a synthetic packed CTA (one CTA, fixed m,n) and verify it writes three per-query, per-head intermediates (max score, log-sum-exp, partial value-weighted sum) to global memory. Compare against a reference numpy/PyTorch attention implementation: rescaling and combining the partials should reproduce the reference attention output to within 1e-2 (fp16).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the shared-memory constraint that limits a CTA's m and n such that (Q tile + K/V tile + intermediate results) at the kernel's data type sizes fit within per-SM shared memory S_smem (e.g., 163 KB per CTA on A100).

verification.spec: For each candidate (m, n) emitted by the offline configuration solver, compute Q_tile + K_tile + V_tile + intermediate bytes at FP16 / FP32 dtypes and verify the sum <= 163 KB (A100 per-CTA shared memory). Verify that (m,n) pairs violating this bound are rejected by the solver.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the per-thread and total register constraint enforcing R_thr(m, n) <= S_reg_thr (typically 255 32-bit registers per thread) and that aggregate registers across concurrent CTAs on an SM do not exceed S_register.

verification.spec: For each candidate (m, n), verify offline-measured R_thr(m,n) <= 255 and that R_CTA(m,n) * C (number of concurrent resident CTAs) <= S_register for the target SM. Verify that configurations violating either bound are excluded from the feasible set.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to obtain per-thread register usage R_thr(m, n) and per-CTA register usage R_CTA(m, n) for each candidate (m, n) via offline compilation and static analysis rather than analytic estimation.

verification.spec: Inspect the offline configuration tool to confirm it compiles each candidate (m,n) kernel via nvcc and parses register usage with cuobjdump/ptxas to obtain R_thr(m,n) and R_CTA(m,n) instead of using analytic formulas. Run on at least one (m,n) and verify reported numbers come from compiled artifacts.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the high-bandwidth lower bound D_flight = S * C * n * h * b >= L * B, enforcing a minimum KV tile size n to keep enough data in flight to cover inherent global memory latency.

verification.spec: Run the offline configuration solver and for each candidate n verify D_flight = S * C * n * h * b >= L * B (with S = #SMs, C = concurrent CTAs per SM at chosen tile, h = head dim, b = KV dtype bytes; L = inherent memory latency ns, B = sustained bandwidth bytes/ns). Verify n values below this bound are rejected.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the CUTLASS MMA constraint requiring both m and n to be powers of two and at least 16 (for the FP16 datatype used in evaluation).

verification.spec: For each candidate (m, n) produced by the offline solver for FP16 dtype, assert m and n are powers of two and m >= 16 and n >= 16. Verify that configurations like (8,16) or (16,24) are rejected.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so the feasible (m, n) set for A100-SXM4-80GB matches the configuration set shown in Figure 8b, including at least the tile pairs that pass the constraints (e.g., (8,16), (8,32), (16,16), (16,32), (16,64), (16,128), (32,16), (32,32), (32,64), (32,128), (64,32), (64,64), (64,128)).

verification.spec: Inspect the compiled multi-tile kernel registry (e.g., the dispatch table mapping (m,n) -> kernel symbol) on A100. Assert the set of registered (m,n) pairs contains at least {(8,16),(8,32),(16,16),(16,32),(16,64),(16,128),(32,16),(32,32),(32,64),(32,128),(64,32),(64,64),(64,128),(128,16),(128,32),(128,64)} matching the checkmarks in Figure 8b. Use importlib to import the kernel module and enumerate available (m,n) tile configs.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that re-running the constraint solver on H100-SXM-80GB removes the (64,32) and (64,64) configurations from the A100 set, yielding the H100 equivalent kernel set.

verification.spec: Run the offline constraint solver / kernel-config enumeration on H100. Verify that (64,32) and (64,64) are NOT in the resulting feasible (m,n) set, while configurations like (16,16),(16,32),(16,64),(16,128),(32,16),(32,32),(32,64),(32,128),(64,128) remain (per Figure 9). Import the H100 kernel dispatch table and assert membership/non-membership.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the runtime tile selector's Q-tile rule: choose the smallest feasible m such that m >= q (the CTA's query count) to avoid splitting the query while not over-allocating on-chip memory.

verification.spec: Invoke the tile selector function with synthetic CTAs having query counts q in {1,5,20,33,65}. Assuming feasible q-tile sizes are {16,32,64,128}, assert it returns m=16 for q in {1,5}, m=32 for q=20, m=64 for q=33, m=128 for q=65. Confirm the rule is 'smallest m in feasible q-tiles with m >= q' rather than nearest power-of-two or fixed value.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the runtime tile selector's KV-tile rule: profile candidate n values offline by sweeping KV length to derive the largest performance-equivalent tile sizes, encoded as a piecewise decision tree, and used as a constant-time lookup per CTA at runtime.

verification.spec: Locate the tile-size selector implementation in the repo. Verify the existence of an offline profiling script that sweeps KV length over feasible n values to derive the largest performance-equivalent n per KV-length range, and that the result is stored as a piecewise decision tree / lookup table consulted in O(1) per CTA at runtime. Instantiate the selector and call its KV-tile lookup for a series of KV lengths (e.g., 128, 256, 1024, 8192); confirm it returns a single n value per call in constant time without invoking profiling.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so the kernel uses cp_async for global-to-shared memory transfers together with double buffering to overlap data movement with computation.

verification.spec: Grep the CUTLASS/CuTe kernel source (under csrc/ or equivalent) for `cp_async` (or `cp.async` PTX / `cute::copy` with async copy atom) usage on the global-to-shared K/V tile loads, and for double-buffering indicators such as multiple shared-memory stages (e.g., `kStages>=2`, ping-pong buffer indices). Both must be present in the multi-tile attention kernel.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so intermediate results (max score, log-sum-exp accumulator, partial value-weighted sum) stored to global memory use FP32 precision for numerical accuracy.

verification.spec: Inspect kernel/merge code to confirm that per-CTA partial outputs (max score, log-sum-exp, partial value-weighted sum) are stored in FP32 (e.g., `float`, `fp32`, `cutlass::float32_t`) in the global-memory scratch buffers, regardless of FP16 KV input. Verify by reading the merge kernel's global-memory load types and the partial-output write types in the forward kernel.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Forward stage (multi-stream forward and long-KV split) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented so that CTAs with the same (m, n) configuration execute sequentially within one stream, while CTAs with different configurations execute in parallel across streams (allowing the launch overhead of subsequent kernels to overlap with execution of preceding kernels).

verification.spec: Locate the multi-stream forward dispatcher in PAT. Construct a synthetic batch whose CTAs span two distinct (m,n) configurations. Verify in code that one cudaStream_t is created per distinct (m,n) and that CTAs are enqueued grouped by (m,n) into that stream. Run with CUDA profiling (e.g., nsys or CUPTI) and confirm kernels with different (m,n) overlap in time on different streams, while kernels with the same (m,n) serialize on one stream.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the long-KV split strategy: any CTA whose KV length exceeds the mean KV length across all CTAs in a batch is split along the KV dimension into equal parts so that each piece's KV length is at or below the batch mean.

verification.spec: Construct a synthetic CTA list with KV lengths [100, 200, 400, 8000] (mean = 2175). Invoke the long-KV split routine and assert: (1) CTAs with KV length <= 2175 are unchanged; (2) the 8000-length CTA is split into equal pieces each with KV length <= 2175 (e.g., 4 parts of 2000 each); (3) the union of split pieces preserves total KV length. Repeat for mixed batches with KV lengths {200,300,500,32000}.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the per-CTA partial outputs generated by long-KV split are properly merged together in the merge stage (consistent with the online-softmax merge kernel of Section 7).

verification.spec: Construct a decode batch with at least one CTA whose KV length exceeds the batch mean (triggering long-KV split). Run PAT's attention kernel and compare its final output for each query against a reference attention implementation (e.g., naive PyTorch attention) over the same Q, K, V. Outputs must match within FP16 numerical tolerance (max abs diff < 5e-3), confirming the merge stage correctly combines split partial outputs via online softmax.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Merge stage (online-softmax output merge) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented for a lightweight CUTLASS/CuTe merge kernel that loads per-CTA, per-query, per-head intermediates (max score, log-sum-exp accumulator, partial value-weighted sum) from global memory.

verification.spec: Locate the merge kernel source (CUTLASS/CuTe). Verify it (a) is implemented with CUTLASS/CuTe primitives, (b) loads three per-CTA per-query per-head intermediates (max, lse, partial value sum) from global memory pointers passed as kernel arguments. Confirm via reading kernel signature and the load instructions inside the kernel.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the online-softmax reduction across CTAs (per-query, per-head) that combines partial max scores and log-sum-exp accumulators to produce the correct rescaled softmax denominators.

verification.spec: Implement a reference numpy/PyTorch attention computation on a small Q (e.g., batch=2, heads=4, head_dim=128, kv_len=512) and compare against PAT's pipeline that splits the KV across multiple CTAs and merges with the online-softmax merge kernel. Assert the merged output matches the reference with max abs error < 1e-2 (FP16) and that the rescaled denominators reproduce log-sum-exp combination correctly when CTAs produce disjoint KV partitions.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so the merge kernel normalizes the accumulated value-weighted sum, concatenates all heads, and writes the final query output back to global memory.

verification.spec: Run the PAT merge kernel on synthetic intermediates produced by multiple CTAs covering the same query split across heads. Verify that the kernel (a) normalizes the value-weighted sum by the merged denominator (online softmax) and (b) writes a contiguous concatenated multi-head output to global memory. Compare against a Python reference online-softmax merge; max abs diff < 1e-3 in FP16.

sub_tasks.requirements: Baseline attention kernels and their integrations have been set up for comparison.
Code has been set up to run FlashAttention v2.5.9 (one-query-per-CTA, fixed tile (64, 128)) as a baseline for both the kernel benchmark and the end-to-end serving benchmark.

verification.spec: Verify the benchmark environment installs flash-attn==2.5.9 (pip show flash_attn). Run both the kernel benchmark harness and the end-to-end vLLM serving with `VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASH_ATTN`; both should complete and emit latency numbers for FlashAttention as a baseline.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to run FlashInfer v0.2.5 (query-centric, decoding tile (16, 128) with dynamic CTA partitioning) as a baseline.

verification.spec: Verify flashinfer==0.2.5 is installed. Run the kernel benchmark with FlashInfer's decoding kernel using tile config (16,128) and confirm it produces latency results. Also verify it is selectable as a vLLM attention backend (e.g., VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASHINFER) in the end-to-end harness.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to run FastTree (KV-centric, compute-oriented cost model, tile configs (64,32) and (16,32)) as a baseline.

verification.spec: Confirm the benchmark repo includes FastTree integration (source or submodule) with tile configs (64,32) and (16,32). Run the kernel benchmark for at least one (B,L) configuration with a supported head config (32/32) and confirm FastTree latency is reported.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to run RelayAttention as a baseline (KV-centric; packs first-level shared prefix into CTAs and reuses FlashAttention's kernel for forward).

verification.spec: Confirm RelayAttention is integrated as a baseline. Run the kernel benchmark on a configuration with a single first-level shared prefix (e.g., B=[1,N]) and verify RelayAttention produces a result, packing the first-level shared prefix into a CTA and delegating the forward to FlashAttention's kernel.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the RelayAttention++ extension that stores shared KV blocks from non-first-level prefixes in the same physical space so redundant KV loads benefit from L2 cache.

verification.spec: Inspect the RelayAttention++ baseline implementation and verify that non-first-level prefix KV blocks across requests are mapped to the same physical KV-cache region (deduplicated). Run a decode batch with multi-level shared prefixes and use ncu or instrumentation to confirm that the L2 hit rate for shared prefix KV loads is materially higher than vanilla RelayAttention (improvement matching the 67.4% latency cut reported in Section 8.3).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to run DeFT as a baseline (KV-centric, aggregates queries with shared KV and adjusts the KV length per CTA for load balance; fixed tile (32, 16)).

verification.spec: Confirm DeFT is integrated with fixed tile (32,16) and load-balancing across CTAs. Run the kernel benchmark for a configuration with shared prefixes and uneven KV lengths and verify DeFT produces latency numbers.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to run Cascade Inference as a baseline (KV-centric, packs prefixes into CTAs with fixed settings).

verification.spec: Confirm Cascade Inference (from FlashInfer or standalone) is wired in as a baseline that packs shared prefixes into CTAs with fixed settings. Run the kernel benchmark on a shared-prefix configuration and verify Cascade Inference produces latency output.

sub_tasks.requirements: The end-to-end serving workloads and metrics (Sections 8.4-8.5) have been set up.
Code has been set up to run vLLM v0.9.0 with PAT enabled as the attention backend and to evaluate it against the same vLLM-integrated baselines (FlashAttention, FlashInfer, RelayAttention++).

verification.spec: Verify vllm==0.9.0 is installed. Launch vLLM with `VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=PREFIX_ATTN VLLM_USE_V1=0 vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3-8B --enable-prefix-caching --enforce-eager` and confirm the server starts and serves a sample request. Repeat with backends FLASH_ATTN, FLASHINFER, and RELAY_ATTN (or equivalent) and confirm each starts successfully.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been set up to sweep client request rate across the values used in Figure 12 (approximately 4, 6, 8, 10 reqs/s) for the end-to-end experiment.

verification.spec: Inspect the end-to-end benchmark driver script (e.g., run_e2e_bench_*.sh or its Python entry point) and confirm it iterates request-rate values covering approximately {4, 6, 8, 10} reqs/s. Verify by grepping for the rate list and by running with a smoke-test rate to confirm the rate is applied to the load generator.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to measure and report mean TTFT, mean TPOT, and P99 TPOT per (model, trace, request rate, baseline) cell.

verification.spec: Run a short end-to-end benchmark (one model, one trace, one request rate, one backend) and verify the output report contains numerical fields for mean TTFT, mean TPOT, and P99 TPOT. Spot-check the computation: TTFT = time_first_token - request_arrival; TPOT averaged over inter-token intervals; P99 TPOT = 99th percentile across all output tokens.
